title: Poulet Basquaise
subtitle: Basque chicken with peppers and tomatoes
excerpt: Chicken thighs braised in a warm, peppery sauce of softened peppers, tomatoes, white wine, and smoked paprika. A dish from the French Basque country with Iberian flavours.
sourceNotes: Poulet à la basquaise is documented in Julia Child, Louisette Bertholle, and Simone Beck, Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) and Frédérick E. Grasser-Hermé, La Cuisine du Pays Basque (2004). The Basque Country (on both the French and Spanish sides) shares this dish, with minor variations in the type of pepper used.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Use a larger casserole.

content.text: Poulet basquaise is a one-pot braise: everything goes into the casserole in sequence, the lid goes on, and the chicken cooks in the fragrant steam of peppers, tomatoes, and wine. The key step is browning the chicken properly before the braising begins — the deep gold crust gives the sauce its body and colour.

The dish has a mild heat from the peppers and a quiet smokiness from the paprika. The Basque version uses an Espelette pepper (a dried chilli grown in the Basque Country); smoked paprika is the accessible substitute that achieves a similar result.

Season the chicken thighs. Heat the olive oil in a large casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the thighs skin-side down for 5 to 6 minutes until the skin is deep gold and crisp. Turn and brown the other side for 3 minutes. Transfer to a plate.

In the same casserole, cook the onion for 8 minutes until soft. Add the garlic and paprika, stir for 1 minute. Add the peppers and cook for 5 minutes. Add the wine and let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add the tinned tomatoes, thyme, and bay leaves. Return the chicken thighs to the casserole, skin-side up. Cover and cook over medium-low heat for 30 to 35 minutes until the chicken is cooked through. Remove the lid for the last 10 minutes to let the skin crisp slightly. Season and scatter with parsley.

items.cause: Steam from the lid condenses and softens the skin
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 10 minutes; this allows the skin to dry and crisp

items.cause: Too much liquid from the tinned tomatoes and wine
items.fix: Cook uncovered for longer; crush some of the tomatoes against the pan to release their pulp and help the sauce thicken

content.text: Poulet basquaise sits at the intersection of French and Spanish cooking — the Basque Country straddles the border and its cuisine reflects both. The pepper-tomato combination (piperade) and the smoked paprika are Iberian in spirit; the wine-braised one-pot method is firmly French. It is summer food: made when peppers are good and cheap, served with crusty bread or rice.
